Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Nominal compound: murrinh ‘speech/language’ + patha ‘good’; used as a compact evaluative expression.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Good, straight, appropriate speech; language used in a socially fitting way.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Murrinh-ka patha; the speech is good and appropriate.
Usage Contexts: daily speech; language learning; classroom; family correction
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Built from the language name itself, commonly glossed as ‘language-good’; treat as high-confidence lexical-cultural expression, not a proverb.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Nominal/adjectival phrase: murrinh ‘speech’ + thathpirr ‘true/real’; fixed evaluative phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: The real story; truthful talk; a statement that should be trusted.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Murrinh-ka thathpirr; the statement is true.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Attested phrase pattern in public Murrinhpatha religious and narrative text; useful for truth/evidence modeling.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Body-part plus adjective phrase: marda ‘heart/inside feeling’ + thathpirr ‘true/real’; metaphorical affective noun phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Deep sincerity, real affection, or wholehearted commitment.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ngatha marda patha; I really feel good toward it/them.
Usage Contexts: family; care; condolence; praise; church; reconciliation
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Murrinhpatha public texts use marda in affective contexts; figurative body-part expression requires speaker validation.
Third Example Sentence: Ngay marda thathpirr ngarra kardu nhini-yu.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Locative directional clause with ngarra ‘toward/at’ + da ‘place/country/home’ + finite motion verb.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To return to one’s place, home, family ground, or proper position.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ngay-ka da ngay-nu ngurran; I am going to my place.
Usage Contexts: travel; homecoming; kinship; school; everyday movement
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Phrasebook examples gloss this as ‘I’m going home’; included as an idiomatic cultural-place expression rather than an opaque idiom.
Third Example Sentence: Palngun ngarra da ngurran da patha-nu.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Locative phrase: da ‘place/country’ + ngarra locative marker + putek ‘earth/ground/world’; place-reference construction.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: This world, this country, or the shared ground people live on.
Usage Contexts: country; environment; teaching; ceremony-adjacent public explanation
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Grounded in recurring Murrinhpatha public text phraseology for land/world; cultural interpretation needs local review.
First Example Sentence: Kardu wurnangat ngarra da putek kanhi.
Third Example Sentence: Murrinh patha ngarra da putek kanhi.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Noun phrase: da ‘place/country/way’ + murntak ‘old/old-time’; temporal-cultural nominal phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: The old ways; ancestral or earlier ways of living and knowing.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Da old-time; the former way or older practice.
Usage Contexts: history; education; family teaching; cultural comparison
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Appears in Murrinhpatha text about schooling and culture; use carefully because it may refer to culturally specific knowledge.
Second Example Sentence: Da murntak-ka murrinh ngarra kardu thangunu.
Third Example Sentence: Nugarn-ka da murntak mampirra kardu kigay-nu.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Purposive noun phrase with borrowed/onomatopoetic food term mi dhepdhep plus purposive -nu.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: For food; used practically, but can carry the broader sense of going for sustenance or provisions.
Usage Contexts: shop; family errands; clinic; travel; daily planning
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Attested in public phrasebook-style examples about going to the shop for food; included as a culturally routine task expression.
First Example Sentence: Kardu pumbankawuk ngarra shop mi dhepdhep-nu.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: From the fire; figuratively, from danger, heat, trouble, or the cause of harm.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Based on published example involving fire-source in Murrinhpatha sentence planning literature; figurative extension is synthetic and needs speaker confirmation.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Animal subject clause with incorporated/complex verb form; full finite clause used expressively.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A loud complaint or noisy warning directed at someone; literally the dog is barking at me.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ku were-ka ngay-nu karduwarlbarl; the dog is making noise at me.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Attested phrasebook example glossed as a dog barking at the speaker; idiomatic social extension should be treated as dataset augmentation.
Third Example Sentence: Kardu kigay-ka ku were dirranngingarlbarl mampirra.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Nominalized/derived speech noun phrase: murrinh ‘language/speech’ + tetemanthay; title-like fixed legal/glossary expression.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Law speech, careful institutional wording, or specialized formal language.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Murrinh law; legal or formal explanatory speech.
Usage Contexts: legal; interpreting; government services; formal meetings
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Attested in the title of a Murrinhpatha–English legal glossary; useful for domain adaptation and register modeling.
